<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:أساكوسا]][[arz:اساكوسا]][[ca:Asakusa]][[cs:Asakusa (Tokio)]][[de:Asakusa]][[en:Asakusa]][[eo:Asakusa]][[es:Asakusa]][[fa:آساکوسا]][[fi:Asakusa]][[fr:Asakusa]][[hu:Aszakusza]][[id:Asakusa]][[it:Asakusa]][[ja:浅草]][[ko:아사쿠사]][[ms:Asakusa]][[nl:Asakusa]][[pl:Asakusa]][[pt:Asakusa]][[ru:Асакуса]][[sl:Asakusa]][[sr:Асакуса (дистрикт)]][[sv:Asakusa]][[th:อาซากูซะ]][[tr:Asakusa]][[uk:Асакуса (Токіо)]][[zh:浅草]]
{{Short description|Tokyo district|qq=}} {{Other uses|qq=}} {{refimprove|date=March 2023|qq=}} [[File:Day2-2_(40909714314).jpg|thumb|The {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Kaminarimon|en|Kaminarimon|qid=Q2378708|lt=Kaminarimon|1=Kaminarimon}}|qq=}}, with its giant {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Chōchin|Chōchin]]|qq=}}, the outer gate of Sensō-ji temple]] [[File:Sensoji at night 5.JPG|thumb|[[Sensō-ji]] at night]] [[File:Kaminarimon from above - 2020 9 11 various 23 56 11 827000.jpeg|thumb|upright|Aerial view of Asakusa]] {{nihongo|'''Asakusa'''|浅草||{{IPA|ja|asakꜜsa|lang|TomJ-Asakusa.ogg|qq=}}|qq=}} is a district in {{ill|Taitō|en|Taitō|qid=Q232641|lt=Taitō|1=Taitō}}, {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, Japan. It is known for the [[Sensō-ji]], a Buddhist temple dedicated to the {{ill|Bodhisattva|en|Bodhisattva|qid=Q178149|lt=Bodhisattva|1=Bodhisattva}} [[Kannon]]. There are several other temples in Asakusa, as well as various festivals, such as the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Sanja Matsuri|en|Sanja Matsuri|qid=Q219122|lt=Sanja Matsuri|1=Sanja Matsuri}}|qq=}}. ==History== [[File:Hiroshige, Night View of Saruwaka-machi.jpg|thumb|Asakusa {{transliteration|ja|roCategoryu-chome|qq=}} was once the biggest theater district in Edo.]] The development of Asakusa as an entertainment district during the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} came about in part because of the neighboring district, Kuramae. Kuramae was a district of storehouses for rice, which was then used as payment for servants of the feudal government. The keepers ({{transliteration|ja|fudasashi|qq=}}) of these storage houses initially stored the rice for a small fee, but over the years began exchanging the rice for money or selling it to local shopkeepers at a margin.<ref>{{cite web|title=Asakusa: The Heart of Old Tokyo|website=furthereast.com|url=http://www.furthereast.com/travel/asakusa/ |access-date=2015-03-06|archive-date=2015-04-03|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150403022450/http://www.furthereast.com/travel/asakusa/|url-status=dead|qq=}}</ref> Through such trading, many {{transliteration|ja|fudasashi|qq=}} came to have a considerable amount of disposable income and as result theaters and {{ill|okiya|en|okiya|qid=Q2017260|lt=geisha houses|1=Okiya}} began to spring up in nearby Asakusa. For most of the 20th century, Asakusa remained a major entertainment district in Tokyo. The {{transliteration|ja|roCategoryu|qq=}} or "Sixth District" was in particular famous as a theater district, featuring famous cinemas such as the {{ill|Denkikan|en|Denkikan|qid=Q5257897|lt=Denkikan|1=Denkikan}}. The golden years of Asakusa are vividly portrayed in {{ill|Yasunari Kawabata|en|Yasunari Kawabata|qid=Q43736|lt=Yasunari Kawabata|1=Yasunari Kawabata}}'s novel ''{{ill|The Scarlet Gang of Asakusa|en|The Scarlet Gang of Asakusa|qid=Q7762423|lt=The Scarlet Gang of Asakusa|1=The Scarlet Gang of Asakusa}}'' (1930). The area was heavily damaged by US bombing raids during World War II, particularly the {{ill|Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|en|Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)|qid=Q51881408|lt=10 March 1945 firebombing of Tokyo|1=Bombing of Tokyo (10 March 1945)}}. The area was rebuilt after the war, but has now been surpassed by {{ill|Shinjuku|en|Shinjuku|qid=Q179645|lt=Shinjuku|1=Shinjuku}} and other colorful areas in the city in its role as a pleasure district. Asakusa was a ward of {{ill|Tokyo City|en|Tokyo City|qid=Q1207735|lt=Tokyo City|1=Tokyo City}}. In 1947, when the city was transformed into a {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=metropolis|1=Tokyo}}, it was merged with {{ill|Shitaya|en|Shitaya|qid=Q11361329|lt=Shitaya|1=Shitaya}} to form the modern Taito ward. The former ward encompassed 19 neighborhoods in the eastern half of Taitō. ==Geography== Asakusa is on the north-east fringe of central Tokyo, at the eastern end of the {{ill|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|en|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|qid=Q1073046|lt=Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|1=Ginza Line}} subway, approximately one mile east of the major {{STN|Ueno|qq=}} railway/subway interchange. It is central to the area colloquially referred to as [[:en:Shitamachi|Shitamachi]], which literally means "low city," referring to the low elevation of this old part of Tokyo, on the banks of the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|qid=Q222149|lt=Sumida River|1=Sumida River}}. As the name suggests, the area has a more traditionally Japanese atmosphere than some other neighborhoods in Tokyo do. ==Food and drink== [[File:Yōshū Chikanobu Bell.jpg|thumb|Two geisha relaxing after having entertained; the insets showing the curfew bell at Asakusa. [[Ukiyo-e]] woodblock print by {{ill|Toyohara Chikanobu|en|Toyohara Chikanobu|qid=Q3087294|lt=Yōshū Chikanobu|1=Toyohara Chikanobu}}, 1888]] [[File:Sensoji at night.JPG|thumb|Temple in Asakusa]] Asakusa has many restaurants and places to try traditional Japanese foods. One of the most popular treats is {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:satsuma imo|satsuma imo]]|qq=}}, sweet potatoes. Another special treat is {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Kamaboko|en|Kamaboko|qid=Q166204|lt=chikuwa kamaboko|1=Kamaboko}}|qq=}}, grilled fish cakes. The Suzuhiro store serves local craft beer with traditional {{transliteration|ja|kamaboko|qq=}}. Asakusa is also known for spices such as {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Shichimi|en|Shichimi|qid=Q1148919|lt=Shichimi|1=Shichimi}}|qq=}} and {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Sanshō (spice)|sanshō]]|qq=}}. In a city where there are very few buildings older than 50 years because of the [[:en:Bombing of Tokyo in World War II|wartime bombing]], Asakusa has a greater concentration of buildings from the 1950s and 1960s than most other areas in Tokyo do. There are traditional {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Ryokan|en|Ryokan|qid=Q562930|lt=ryokan|1=Ryokan}}|qq=}} (guest-houses) and small-scale apartment buildings throughout the district. In keeping with a peculiarly Tokyo tradition, Asakusa hosts a major cluster of domestic kitchenware stores on {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Kappabashi-dori|en|Kappabashi-dori|qid=Q2977163|lt=Kappabashi-dori|1=Kappabashi-dori}}|qq=}}, which is visited by many Tokyoites for essential supplies. Next to the [[Sensō-ji]] temple grounds is a small amusement park called {{ill|Hanayashiki|en|Hanayashiki|qid=Q863180|lt=Hanayashiki|1=Hanayashiki}}, which claims to be the oldest amusement park in Japan. The neighborhood theaters specialize in showing classic Japanese films, as many of the tourists are elderly Japanese. Cruises down the {{ill|Sumida River|en|Sumida River|qid=Q222149|lt=Sumida River|1=Sumida River}} depart from a wharf a five-minute walk from the temple. Asakusa is Tokyo's oldest {{ill|Geisha|en|Geisha|qid=Q82723|lt=Geisha|1=Geisha}} district, and still has 45 actively working geisha.{{Citation needed|date=February 2021|qq=}} Because of its colourful location, downtown credentials, and relaxed atmosphere by Tokyo standards, Asakusa is a popular accommodation choice for budget travelers. ==Carnival== The neighborhood is famous for its annual Brazilian style {{ill|carnival|en|carnival|qid=Q4618|lt=carnival|1=Carnival}}. There is a significant Brazilian presence in the local community and the Association of [[:en:Samba School|Samba School]]s of Asakusa is based there.<ref>[http://www.tamborins.com.br/asulti/exibe.php?selec1=asulti&selec2=&selec3=20050826002 Tamborins.com.br (in Portuguese)] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071009223606/http://www.tamborins.com.br/asulti/exibe.php?selec1=asulti&selec2=&selec3=20050826002 |date=2007-10-09 |qq=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |url=https://unseendestination.com/asia/top-five-tourist-attractions-in-tokyo/ |title=Senso-Ji Temple-Tokyo: 5 Top Amazing Attractions Places To Visit |access-date=12 May 2020 |archive-date=23 September 2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200923092511/https://unseendestination.com/asia/top-five-tourist-attractions-in-tokyo/ |url-status=dead |qq=}}</ref> == {{transl|ja|Sanja Matsuri|qq=}} == Although there are many festivals throughout the year in Asakusa, the most famous of them is the {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Sanja Matsuri|en|Sanja Matsuri|qid=Q219122|lt=Sanja Matsuri|1=Sanja Matsuri}}|qq=}}, also known as Sanja Festival, which takes place in May.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/tokyo/asakusa/information|title=About Asakusa – Tokyo Travel Guide {{!|qq=}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-06-17|qq=}}</ref> In this festival, {{transliteration|ja|[[mikoshi]]|qq=}} (portable shrines) and floats are pulled through the streets while loud shouts accompany them, and during the festival's 3 days, 1.5 million people come out to celebrate.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://planetyze.com/en/japan/tokyo/sanja-festival/information|title=About Sanja Matsuri (Sanja Festival) – Tokyo Travel Guide {{!|qq=}} Planetyze|website=Planetyze|language=en|access-date=2017-06-17|qq=}}</ref> ==Transportation== The district has two railway stations with the same name: *[[:en:Asakusa Station (Tokyo Metro, Toei, Tobu)|Asakusa Station]] {{Plainlist| * {{TBLS|TS|qq=}} {{ill|Tobu Skytree Line|en|Tobu Skytree Line|qid=Q13360900|lt=Tobu Skytree Line|1=Tobu Skytree Line}} (TS-01) * {{TSLS|A|qq=}} {{ill|Toei Asakusa Line|en|Toei Asakusa Line|qid=Q720653|lt=Toei Asakusa Line|1=Toei Asakusa Line}} (A-18) * {{TSLS|G|qq=}} {{ill|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|en|Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|qid=Q1073046|lt=Tokyo Metro Ginza Line|1=Ginza Line}} (G-19) }} *{{ill|Asakusa Station (Tsukuba Express)|en|Asakusa Station (Tsukuba Express)|qid=Q4803450|lt=Asakusa Station|1=Asakusa Station (Tsukuba Express)}} {{Plainlist| * [[File:Tsukuba Express mark.svg|18px]] {{ill|Tsukuba Express|en|Tsukuba Express|qid=Q1377699|lt=Tsukuba Express|1=Tsukuba Express}} (TX-03) }} ==Education== [[File:台東区立浅草中学校.jpg|thumb|{{ill|Asakusa Junior High School|ja|台東区立浅草中学校}]]] Taitō City Board of Education operates public elementary and junior high schools. Asakusa 1-{{transliteration|ja|[[:en:Chome|Chome]]|qq=}} and portions of 2-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} are zoned to {{ill|Asakusa Elementary School|ja|台東区立浅草小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakusa Elementary School|1=Asakusa Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q81916469}}. All of 6- and 7-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} and parts of 3-, 4-, and 5-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} are zoned to {{ill|Fuji Elementary School|ja|台東区立富士小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fuji Elementary School|1=Fuji Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11411953}}. Parts of 3-, 4-, and 5-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} are zoned to {{ill|Senzoku Elementary School|ja|千束小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senzoku Elementary School|qq=}}. Portions of 2-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} are zoned to {{ill|Kinryu Elementary School|ja|台東区立金竜小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinryu Elementary School|1=Kinryu Elementary School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11411961}}. Portions of 5-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} are zoned to {{ill|Higashi-Asakusa Elementary School|ja|東浅草小学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi-Asakusa Elementary School|qq=}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.city.taito.lg.jp/kosodatekyouiku/kuritsushocyugaCategoryo/nyugakutugaku/tsuugakukuiki.files/taitoukuritsusyougaCategoryou_tsuugakukuiki.pdf|title=台東区立小学校通学区域表|publisher=City of Taito|access-date=2022-10-09|qq=}}</ref> Asakusa 1- and 2-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} are zoned to {{ill|Asakusa Junior High School|ja|台東区立浅草中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asakusa Junior High School|1=Asakusa Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11411958}}. All of Asakusa 6- and 7-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} and portions of 3-, 4-, and 5-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} are zoned to {{ill|Sakurabashi Junior High School|ja|桜橋中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sakurabashi Junior High School|qq=}}. Portions of Asakusa 3-, 4-, and 5-{{transliteration|ja|chome|qq=}} are zoned to {{ill|Hakuyo Junior High School|ja|台東区立柏葉中学校|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakuyo Junior High School|1=Hakuyo Junior High School|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q79163351}}.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.city.taito.lg.jp/kosodatekyouiku/kuritsushocyugaCategoryo/nyugakutugaku/tsuugakukuiki.files/taitoukuritsutyuugaCategoryou_tsuugakukuiki.pdf|title=台東区立中学校通学区域表|publisher=City of Taito|access-date=2022-10-09|qq=}}</ref> == In art and literature == * Kawabata Yasunari, ''{{ill|The Scarlet Gang of Asakusa|en|The Scarlet Gang of Asakusa|qid=Q7762423|lt=The Scarlet Gang of Asakusa|1=The Scarlet Gang of Asakusa}}'' (1930) * Kankichi Ryotsu, protagonist of the popular anime and manga series {{transliteration|ja|[[:en:KochiKame|KochiKame]]|qq=}}, is born in Asakusa. * "Corn Dog," season 1, episode 2 of ''Midnight Diner, Tokyo Stories'', a Netflix original series (2016), is about an old comedian who works in Asakusa and his successful young protégé. * The anime {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Sarazanmai|en|Sarazanmai|qid=Q55074957|lt=Sarazanmai|1=Sarazanmai}}|qq=}} is set in Asakusa. * In the anime and manga series ''{{ill|Fire Force|en|Fire Force|qid=Q24636973|lt=Fire Force|1=Fire Force}}'', Asakusa shows up as the district under the jurisdiction of the Special Fire Force Company 7 and is the setting of the Asakusa arc. * In the popular anime and manga series, ''{{ill|Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba|en|Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba|qid=Q24862683|lt=Demon Slayer|1=Demon Slayer: Kimetsu no Yaiba}}'', chapters 14–17 and episodes 7–10, the primary location is Taishō-period Asakusa. ==See also== {{Portal|Tokyo|qq=}} * {{ill|Asakusa Culture Tourist Information Center|en|Asakusa Culture Tourist Information Center|qid=Q24877101|lt=Asakusa Culture Tourist Information Center|1=Asakusa Culture Tourist Information Center}} * [[Asakusa Shrine]] * {{ill|Hanayashiki|en|Hanayashiki|qid=Q863180|lt=Hanayashiki|1=Hanayashiki}}, oldest amusement park in Japan. * {{transliteration|ja|{{ill|Hōzōmon|en|Hōzōmon|qid=Q2378896|lt=Hōzōmon|1=Hōzōmon}}|qq=}} *
